evidence: Packages map tightly to domain capabilities: api (HTTP), config (grammar/relation config), exploration (semantic analysis), model (DTOs), query (corpus execution), tagging (POS), utils (math/CQL helpers), viz (SVG generation) — no catch-all or historically-named packages.

WordSketchApiServer.start() is a pure routing façade: it creates contexts and delegates every handler to either SketchHandlers or ExplorationHandlers with no business logic in the server class itself.

SketchHandlers owns word-sketch, concordance, BCQL and radial endpoints; ExplorationHandlers owns all semantic-field exploration endpoints — the split matches the two natural feature domains and localises change.

Config-driven relation design: GrammarConfigLoader.getRelation() + RelationConfig.getFullPattern() means adding a new grammatical relation requires only a JSON change to relations.json, not a code change.

model package cleanly contains all DTOs (ExplorationResult, DiscoveredNoun, CoreCollocate, AdjectiveProfile, ComparisonResult, Edge, ExploreOptions, QueryResults) with no logic from handlers or the query layer bleeding in.

CLAUDE.md API table (lines 70-75) lists only 4 of the 14 endpoints registered in WordSketchApiServer — omits /api/relations, /api/relations/dep, /api/semantic-field, /api/semantic-field/examples, /api/concordance/examples, /api/visual/radial, /api/bcql. A developer reading the docs gets an incomplete and misleading picture of the API surface.

handleSemanticField (ExplorationHandlers line 259) silently accepts both 'seeds' and 'nouns' as parameter aliases via 'params.containsKey("seeds") ? params.get("seeds") : requireParam(exchange, params, "nouns")'. This undocumented shim creates an opaque compatibility alias; callers cannot determine the canonical parameter from docs or code signature.

SemanticFieldExplorer retains a @Deprecated String-path constructor alongside the preferred QueryExecutor constructor, leaving two competing initialization contracts in the exploration package until the old path is removed.

WordSketchApiServer constructor carries a TODO: 'Move HttpServer creation and route registration here so start() only calls server.start()' — an acknowledged split between construction-time and start-time registration that blurs the ownership of server lifecycle.

QueryExecutor interface (query/QueryExecutor.java) has a single production implementation (BlackLabQueryExecutor) but is well-documented and enables testability; executeSurfacePattern Javadoc explicitly notes headPosition/collocatePosition are 'currently unused' — the interface leaks implementation detail through unused parameters that exist only for compatibility

CollocateProfileComparator (exploration/CollocateProfileComparator.java) Javadoc states 'Extracted from SemanticFieldExplorer to keep that class below the 400-line threshold' — this is mechanical class-splitting for metrics, not a genuine domain concept; the class duplicates the ADJECTIVE_PATTERN constant from SemanticFieldExplorer and is only ever instantiated inside SemanticFieldExplorer

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreMultiSeed (line 337) is a single-line pass-through: 'return comparator.exploreMultiSeed(seeds, relationConfig, minLogDice, topCollocates, minShared)' with zero added behaviour — the public API facade adds indirection depth without leverage

ExplorationHandlers (api/ExplorationHandlers.java) stores 'private final QueryExecutor executor' (assigned in constructor, line 40) but never calls any method on it — all execution flows through 'semanticFieldExplorer'; the field is dead constructor cargo

SemanticFieldExplorer has 'private final String indexPath' (line 77) that is always null in the primary DI constructor (line 103) and is never read in any method; it exists only as a relic of the now-deprecated String-path constructor

ExplorationResult (model/ExplorationResult.java) exposes its internals as 'public final' fields (seed, seedCollocates, seedCollocateFrequencies, discoveredNouns, coreCollocates) while sibling ComparisonResult (model/ComparisonResult.java) uses private fields with getters — no documented reason for the inconsistency; callers access 'result.seed' and 'result.seedCollocates' directly

RelationConfig record (config/RelationConfig.java) carries meaningful behaviour: getFullPattern, substituteHeadword, collocatePosGroup, getDeprel — abstraction earns its keep; similarly HttpApiUtils is a focused static utility that consistently enforces CORS + encoding policy across all endpoints
wrapHandler in WordSketchApiServer consistently enforces error-handling policy (IOException and Exception → HTTP 500) — a thin wrapper that earns its existence
CqlUtils provides a single well-defined primitive (splitCqlTokens, escapeForRegex) that genuinely reduces duplication across RelationConfig and GrammarConfigLoader
BlackLabQueryExecutor.executeBcqlQuery is cleanly decomposed into three named phases (collectHits, scoreHits, rank+limit) with private methods carrying clear responsibilities

BlackLabSnippetParser compiles all regex patterns as named class-level constants (LEMMA_ATTR, XPOS_ATTR, SENT_BOUND_LEFT, etc.) and each method does exactly one thing; the class is a focused utility with no mixed concerns
CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens handles nested brackets and escaped quoted strings with a depth-counter loop; local state is minimal and entirely bounded to the method
ExplorationHandlers.formatSeedCollocate / formatDiscoveredNoun / formatCoreCollocate / formatEdge are small, pure, reused helpers extracted consistently across both single- and multi-seed handlers
ConlluConverter.convertConlluToWplChunks uses a clean streaming loop with three pieces of bounded state (inSentence, sentencesInChunk, writer) and lazy file creation

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern is ~140 LOC with four in-method phases; mid-method state includes seedCollocScores, nounProfiles, collocFrequency, collocTotalScore all mutated across separate loops, requiring significant mental stack to hold simultaneously

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExplore and handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti have ~80% overlapping structure (param parsing, grammar-config null guard, relation lookup, ExploreParams parsing, response building, seed_collocates/coreCollocates/edges formatting) with only seed extraction and the explorer call differing

BlackLabQueryExecutor.collectHits initialises leftText/rightText/matchText/xmlSnippet to empty string and then reassigns them only inside a deeply-nested null-guard chain (conc != null → parts != null && length >= 3); the defensive sprawl obscures which paths are actually reachable

GrammarConfigLoader primary constructor is ~100 LOC containing copulas rejection, tokenCount validation with four boolean guards (hasHead, hasDeprel, hasPlaceholder, isDual), position derivation/override, and duplicate-ID checking all inline

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticField uses params.containsKey("seeds") ? params.get("seeds") : requireParam(exchange, params, "nouns"); passing seeds= (empty string) bypasses requireParam's empty-value guard entirely
BlackLabSnippetParser retains an @Deprecated extractCollocateFromMatchText method annotated 'No production callers' — confirmed dead code that adds API surface noise
ExploreOptions record cleanly bundles 7 tuning+positional parameters for the handler→SemanticFieldExplorer boundary, collapsing a previously sprawling argument list into one explicit, named object.
The model package (ExplorationResult, DiscoveredNoun, CoreCollocate, AdjectiveProfile, Edge) defines stable DTOs that cross the exploration→API seam without leaking internals.
HttpApiUtils centralises all HTTP machinery (sendJsonResponse, sendError, sendOptionsResponse, requireParam, parseQueryParams) for both handler classes, keeping the seam clean.
GrammarConfigLoader exposes getRelation(id): Optional<RelationConfig>, a minimal, unsurprising lookup contract that hides its internal map from callers.
WordSketchApiServer is a thin router; SketchHandlers and ExplorationHandlers receive their dependencies via constructor injection and carry no routing logic.

ExplorationResult.empty() initialises seedCollocates/seedCollocateFrequencies/discoveredNouns/coreCollocates as Map.of()/List.of() (never null), but handleSemanticFieldExplore and handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti both null-check every one of those fields before iterating — the handlers do not trust the model's own contract.

handleSemanticFieldExplore and handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti each independently iterate result.seedCollocates, result.coreCollocates, and result.getEdges() into List<Map<String,Object>> using the same private format helpers; the multi-seed variant also iterates result.coreCollocates a second time to build a separate 'common_collocates' (strings-only) list — the ExplorationResult→JSON translation is duplicated across both methods rather than centralised.

In handleSemanticFieldExplore the handler itself computes: String reverseCollocatePattern = relationConfig.get().collocatePosGroup() == PosGroup.ADJ ? "[xpos=\"JJ.*\"]" : "[xpos=\"NN.*|VB.*\"]"; — this PosGroup→CQL-regex translation belongs in RelationConfig (which already owns collocatePosGroup()), not in an HTTP handler.

handleConcordanceExamples silently falls back to a proximity pattern ("word1" []{0,5} "word2") when grammarConfig is null or the relation is absent, returning the fallback response with no field indicating that a completely different search type was used — the API boundary contract is opaque.

The nullable grammarConfig is independently guarded (if grammarConfig == null → sendError 500) in handleSemanticFieldExplore, handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti, and handleRelationsImpl; the WordSketchApiServer constructor logs a warning but does not fail fast, so partial-initialisation state propagates into multiple handlers.

AdjectiveProfile.getStrongestNoun() is declared to return String but uses .orElse(null), so null is a valid return value; callers such as ExplorationHandlers.formatAdjectiveProfile() pass the result directly into a map without a null check

HttpApiUtils.requireParam() is declared to return String but returns null when the parameter is missing (line 98); the javadoc warns callers to check for null but the type system does not enforce this — Optional<String> would encode nullability at the type level

ExplorationHandlers line 100: ((Map<String, Object>) response.get("parameters")).put("nouns_per", ...) is an unchecked cast from Object to Map<String, Object>; the parent map is built in buildBaseExploreResponse and the cast is guaranteed to succeed today but is fragile and generates an unchecked-cast warning

TaggedToken.getPosGroup() bypasses the PosGroup enum for five common categories: returns raw String literals "det", "pron", "prep", "conj", "punct" instead of PosGroup.OTHER or an extended enum value; PosGroup.fromString() is unable to round-trip these values back to enum constants

GrammarConfigLoader.parseRelationType() returns null (declared return type RelationType) when the value is absent or unrecognised; the RelationConfig record field 'RelationType relationType' therefore accepts null silently; SketchHandlers line 95 calls rel.relationType().name() without a null guard, which would throw NPE if any relation lacks a type
Naming is uniformly standard Java throughout all 32 files: camelCase methods/fields, PascalCase classes, UPPER_CASE constants — no snake_case or vocabulary drift anywhere.

model/ package has two distinct type strategies for sibling data-holder classes: AdjectiveProfile, CoreCollocate, DiscoveredNoun, Edge, ExplorationResult all expose data via public final fields, while ComparisonResult uses private fields + getters (getNouns(), getAllAdjectives(), getFullyShared(), getPartiallyShared(), getSpecific(), getSpecificTo(), getEdges()).
ExploreOptions in model/ is a Java record, a third distinct pattern for holding domain data alongside the public-fields and getter-based approaches.

BlackLab indexer implementation is correctly isolated in indexer/blacklab/ (BlackLabConllUIndexer, ConlluConverter). BlackLab query implementation (BlackLabQueryExecutor, BlackLabSnippetParser) sits at the query/ package root alongside the QueryExecutor interface — a structural inconsistency versus the indexer convention.
ADJECTIVE_PATTERN constant ('[xpos="JJ.*"]') is duplicated as a private static final in both SemanticFieldExplorer and CollocateProfileComparator, two sibling classes in the exploration/ package.

api/ package applies consistent package-private visibility for handler classes (ExplorationHandlers, SketchHandlers) with only the facade (WordSketchApiServer) and utilities (HttpApiUtils) being public — consistent across all four api/ files.

TaggedToken in tagging/ uses private fields + getters (getWord(), getLemma(), getTag(), getPosition()), while most model/ domain objects use public final fields — a mild style island in the tagging package.
GET /api/sketch/{lemma} returns top-level key 'patterns' while GET /api/sketch/{lemma}/dep returns 'relations' — parallel endpoints with different shapes (SketchHandlers.java lines 175-178)
/api/semantic-field accepts both 'seeds' and 'nouns' as input parameter aliases (ExplorationHandlers.java lines 259-261); no other endpoint has dual parameter names
handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti puts both 'seeds' (input set) and 'seed' (result.seed) in the same response (ExplorationHandlers.java lines 198-200), making the multi-seed response shape ambiguous

handleConcordanceExamples emits 'highlighted' and 'sentence' with identical values — exMap.put("highlighted", r.getSentence()) mirrors exMap.put("sentence", r.getSentence()) (SketchHandlers.java lines 297-298)
POST /api/bcql response conditionally includes 'raw', 'collocate_lemma', 'frequency', 'log_dice' fields only when non-null (SketchHandlers.java lines 375-384), making the response shape unpredictable
QueryExecutor.executeSurfacePattern declares headPosition and collocatePosition parameters documented as 'currently unused', yet they remain required at every call site (QueryExecutor.java)
AdjectiveProfile public constructor takes 10 positional parameters with no builder pattern, creating a fragile wide API surface (AdjectiveProfile.java)

Surface sketch per-relation data includes 'cql' and 'collocate_pos_group' fields; DEP sketch data includes 'deprel' and 'total_matches' but not 'cql' — sibling structures share only 'name' and 'collocations' (SketchHandlers.java handleFullSketchImpl)
QueryExecutor.executeSurfacePattern documents headPosition and collocatePosition as 'currently unused' but retains them for 'interface compatibility', making the interface contract misleading
GrammarConfigLoader uses throwMissing(Path) — a method declared to return String but always throws — purely to enable constructor chaining inside a ternary; indirect and surprising to readers

SemanticFieldExplorer carries an ownsExecutor boolean and indexPath field purely to support the @Deprecated constructor path; the DI constructor sets indexPath to null, meaning a field that is always null in normal usage

RelationConfig.posGroupFromConstraint is 20+ lines of chained String.contains checks repeated once for xpos= and again for tag=, with logger.warn() calls inlined into the return lines; the two-pass structure obscures the simple POS→enum mapping
Model classes (AdjectiveProfile, ComparisonResult, CoreCollocate, ExplorationResult, Edge) use clear final fields, focused constructors, and helper query methods with no leaking concerns
PosGroup enum is a clean, direct definition with a well-behaved fromString factory
QueryExecutor interface has thorough Javadoc that explicitly distinguishes findCollocations, executeBcqlQuery, and executeSurfacePattern to prevent misuse
ExplorationHandlers correctly uses a private record ExploreParams and parseExploreParams to avoid repeating parameter parsing logic across two handler methods
CqlUtils is a focused utility final class with a single named primitive splitCqlTokens and escapeForRegex; its own Javadoc notes the boundary with GrammarConfigLoader
Package decomposition is clean: api/, config/, exploration/, model/, query/, indexer/, tagging/, utils/, viz/ each have a coherent single domain responsibility.
Model classes (AdjectiveProfile, ComparisonResult, CoreCollocate, ExplorationResult) are pure data holders — no service logic bleeds into them.

HttpApiUtils has tightly-scoped utility methods (sendJsonResponse, sendError, sendOptionsResponse, sendBinaryResponse, requireMethod, requireParam, parseQueryParams), each doing exactly one HTTP-level thing.
WordSketchApiServer delegates all domain logic to SketchHandlers and ExplorationHandlers; the server itself only wires routes and provides error-wrapping infrastructure.

QueryExecutor.executeSurfacePattern declares headPosition and collocatePosition parameters but its own javadoc says 'currently unused'; BlackLabQueryExecutor ignores them and re-derives collocate position from the pattern string. Every call site must pass dummy values that mean nothing.

handleSemanticFieldExplore and handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti both contain four structurally identical for-loops that convert ExplorationResult sub-collections into response maps (seedCollocs, nouns, coreCollocs, edges). ~80 lines of near-verbatim duplication exist between the two handlers.

handleSemanticFieldExplore assembles the BCQL pattern, reverse collocate pattern, head/collocate positions, and ExploreOptions entirely inside the HTTP handler, rather than delegating this to SemanticFieldExplorer or RelationConfig. The handler must understand domain internals (collocatePosGroup, getFullPattern, headPosition, collocatePosition) that belong in the service layer.

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern (~130 lines) interleaves algorithm steps with dense debug-log blocks; the logging is not extractable without also extracting the data, coupling presentation (log output) with computation in a way that makes the algorithm harder to trace.

CollocateProfileComparator was already extracted from SemanticFieldExplorer to keep classes manageable, showing design-awareness, but the class still holds both 'build raw profiles' and 'build profile list' as private methods with a shared mutable state pattern.

WordSketchApiServer.wrapHandler (lines 161-174) provides a uniform last-resort safety net: catches IOException and Exception for every registered handler, sending a 500 response with the exception message

BlackLabQueryExecutor consistently declares `throws IOException` on all public methods and wraps BlackLab's InvalidQuery and other checked exceptions into IOException with cause chain preserved (e.g. line 137: `throw new IOException("BCQL parse error: " + e.getMessage(), e)`)
GrammarConfigLoader consistently throws IOException on all config validation errors, with detailed messages per violation

ExplorationHandlers mixes strategies within the same class: exploreMultiSeed is wrapped in a try-catch with a domain-specific message ("Multi-seed exploration failed: ..."), but exploreByPattern, compareCollocateProfiles, and fetchExamples are called bare — relying silently on wrapHandler for error propagation

SketchHandlers.handleFullSketchImpl swallows per-relation query failures silently: `catch (Exception e) { logger.warn(...) }` causes a failing relation to vanish from the response with no indicator to the caller that a partial result was returned
BlackLabSnippetParser returns null uniformly for all parsing failures without distinguishing between 'not found' and 'malformed input' — callers cannot distinguish absent data from parse error

Main.handleServerCommand wraps executor.close() IOException in a System.err.println during shutdown rather than a proper logger.error call, inconsistent with the rest of the shutdown hook logging pattern

SemanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles (line 295-300) is a single-line pass-through to comparator.compareCollocateProfiles with identical signature and zero added behavior (no logging, defaulting, validation, or enrichment).

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreMultiSeed (line 331-338) is similarly a pure 1-line relay to comparator.exploreMultiSeed; ExplorationHandlers calls both of these directly via the explorer, making SemanticFieldExplorer a partial relay class for 2 of its 5 public methods.

RelationConfig.escapeForRegex(String) (private method) is a zero-behavior single-line wrapper around CqlUtils.escapeForRegex; the private relay buys nothing — CqlUtils could be called directly at the two call sites.
Mechanical scan: 0 delegation-heavy classes detected, consistent with the localized nature of these findings.

SketchHandlers and ExplorationHandlers each hold a QueryExecutor and GrammarConfigLoader and use them directly with genuine added behavior (parameter parsing, error handling, response construction) — no relay pattern.
WordSketchApiServer constructs SemanticFieldExplorer inline and injects it into ExplorationHandlers, adding value through lifecycle management and wiring — not a delegation concern.

QueryExecutor interface has a single implementation (BlackLabQueryExecutor); PosTagger has a single implementation (SimpleTagger). These are single-impl interfaces (interface_honesty concern) but not delegation-density issues.
SemanticFieldExplorer has 8 meaningful unit tests using an in-memory StubExecutor, covering shared/specific/partially-shared collocates, edge weights, null/empty seeds — no real index required.
BlackLabSnippetParser has thorough unit tests for extractLemmaFromMatch and extractPosFromMatch covering null, empty, xpos/upos fallback.
Utility classes (LogDiceCalculatorTest 241 lines, LongIntHashMapTest, PatternSubstitutionTest, SimpleTaggerTest) have substantive tests.
GrammarConfigLoader, RelationsValidationTest, HybridAlgorithmTest cover config/grammar loading with both inline JSON and real default grammar.
HandlersTest covers only invalid-input (HTTP 400) paths for SketchHandlers and ExplorationHandlers using null executor — no happy-path tests exist.
BlackLabQueryExecutorTest is fully @Disabled with TODO comments: 'Requires live BlackLab index — integration tests TBD'. Zero actual test methods exist for the 490-line core query engine.
Five placeholder @Disabled empty classes: E2eTestSuite, RegressionTestSuite, IndexerTestSuite, HybridIndexerTestSuite, VizTestSuite — all with empty bodies and TODO comments.

DependencySketchTest, BlackLabBcqlConcordanceTest, and BlackLabDummyTest each hardcode 'D:\\corpora_philsci\\bi' as INDEX_PATH — not portable and will silently skip everywhere except the original developer's machine.
CollocateProfileComparator (264 lines of sorting/ranking logic) has no dedicated tests.
ExplorationHandlers (454 lines) and SketchHandlers (398 lines) — the entire happy-path request pipeline — are not tested beyond null-executor input validation.

strengths: Package hierarchy maps directly to domain capabilities (api, config, exploration, model, query, tagging, utils, viz) — a new engineer can orient instantly from the package list without reading any code.

Handler decomposition into SketchHandlers and ExplorationHandlers cleanly separates the two major feature domains; adding an endpoint requires touching only one handler class and one registration line in WordSketchApiServer.
WordSketchApiServer is a pure routing façade with no business logic — its only responsibility is creating HTTP contexts and delegating to typed handler objects.

Config-driven relation design makes adding new grammatical relations a data change (relations.json) rather than a code change, and that architectural decision is visible and consistent throughout the query and exploration layers.
The model package owns all DTOs without cross-cutting ownership — no handler or query class owns result types, so DTO changes have predictable blast radius.
high_level_elegance.issue_character: Issues are concentrated in documentation accuracy and minor API contract fuzz rather than in the structural decomposition itself: the CLAUDE.md API table documents a small fraction of the real API surface, and one endpoint has an undocumented parameter alias; these do not invalidate the architecture but erode its trustworthiness as a reference for new engineers.
high_level_elegance.score_rationale: The code architecture is genuinely clean and domain-aligned — package decomposition, handler delegation, config-driven design, and DTO ownership all demonstrate a coherent architectural vision, warranting a strong baseline near 80. The primary drag is that CLAUDE.md, the project's developer guide, lists only 4 of 14 registered endpoints, directly violating the 'reference docs match runtime reality' criterion; a developer relying on it misses most of the API surface entirely. Combined with the undocumented parameter alias in handleSemanticField and the retained deprecated constructor, these gaps in contract honesty pull the score to 67.

strengths: QueryExecutor interface provides a clean, well-documented seam that enables testability without deep indirection
RelationConfig record combines data and domain-specific behaviour (pattern substitution, POS-group derivation, deprel extraction) into one cohesive unit
HttpApiUtils and wrapHandler are thin wrappers that consistently enforce real cross-cutting policy (CORS, error serialisation, method validation) — they pay for themselves
CqlUtils isolates bracket-walking logic shared between multiple consumers without over-generalising
Handler split into SketchHandlers / ExplorationHandlers provides a reasonable size and responsibility boundary
abstraction_fitness.issue_character: Mechanical decomposition and leftover scaffolding — classes split for line-count reasons rather than genuine domain boundaries, dead constructor fields carried forward from refactored deprecated paths, and a public-API facade that is a pure pass-through with no added behaviour.
abstraction_fitness.score_rationale: The codebase sits in the 60-65 band: most abstractions are legitimate and several are well-crafted, but there is a cluster of self-described mechanical extractions (CollocateProfileComparator), dead fields surviving deprecated constructors (indexPath, ExplorationHandlers.executor), and a public facade method (exploreMultiSeed) that adds zero leverage over direct delegation. These are not catastrophic — no god classes, no deep wrapper chains, and the core query/config abstractions are honest — but the pattern of metric-driven decomposition and residual constructor cargo consistently reduces definition directness and interface honesty below what the otherwise healthy design would suggest.

strengths: executeBcqlQuery in BlackLabQueryExecutor uses a deliberate three-phase decomposition (collectHits → scoreHits → rank) with each phase extracted into a focused private method, making the overall flow easy to follow
BlackLabSnippetParser is exemplary: regex patterns are named constants, every method has a single purpose, and the public API vs private helpers are clearly distinguished
CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens manages complex bracket/quote nesting with minimal, well-bounded local state and no defensive sprawl

ExplorationHandlers' formatter helpers (formatSeedCollocate, formatDiscoveredNoun, formatCoreCollocate, formatEdge) are extracted consistently and reused identically across both exploration handler methods
low_level_elegance.issue_character: The issues are concentrated rather than pervasive: one over-long exploration method with too much inline state, one pair of nearly-duplicate handler methods that were never unified beyond a small shared helper, and a few localised defensive-noise patterns; most of the codebase outside these hotspots is clean.
low_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase sits clearly above the 60-point 'significant drag' threshold: the majority of methods are well-sized, extraction style is consistent within subsystems, and there are several genuinely elegant implementations (BlackLabSnippetParser, executeBcqlQuery, CqlUtils). What pulls it below 80 is the unresolved tension in the exploration layer: exploreByPattern accumulates four separate mutation phases inline without a parallel decomposition to the one CollocateProfileComparator achieved, and the two main handler methods expose a clear missed extraction opportunity that creates maintenance symmetry risk. These are not trivial cosmetic issues — a developer changing the response shape for one exploration endpoint must manually mirror the change in the other. A score of 67 reflects solid foundations with a concentrated but consequential low-level elegance deficit in the exploration subsystem.

strengths: ExploreOptions record provides an explicit, minimal, named bundle at the handler→SemanticFieldExplorer boundary.
The model package defines stable, seam-crossing DTOs (ExplorationResult, DiscoveredNoun, Edge, etc.) without exposing exploration internals to the API layer.
HttpApiUtils centralises all HTTP-boundary machinery in one place, eliminating per-handler duplication of JSON serialisation and error formatting.
GrammarConfigLoader exposes a minimal, Optional-returning lookup surface (getRelation) that hides internal storage from callers.
WordSketchApiServer is a thin router with dependency-injected handler objects, producing clean, readable composition at the top of the API seam.
mid_level_elegance.issue_character: The seam problems are localised to the exploration-endpoint boundary: duplicated result-to-response translation, a handler that breaks abstraction by computing CQL regex from a PosGroup enum, a model contract that callers do not trust, and two lifecycle/contract surprises (silent fallback, propagated nullable config) — each a distinct failure mode at the same layer transition.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: The structural foundations are solid: clean DTOs, a thin router, centralised HTTP utilities, and an ExploreOptions record show intentional seam design. However, the exploration endpoint boundary accumulates four distinct glue-code problems — duplicated response assembly, untrusted model nullability contract, a POS-regex computation that belongs in RelationConfig, and a silent concordance fallback — plus a scattered null-guard that should be a startup failure. The combination of good architectural bones with several specific, recurring mid-level seam failures places this in the 60–70 range; the clear structural wins prevent a lower score, but the seam issues are real friction for any developer extending the exploration or concordance paths.

strengths: GrammarConfigLoader.getRelation() returns Optional<RelationConfig>, correctly encoding nullability at the type level for a lookup that may not find a match
Generics are applied consistently throughout: no raw List or Map without type parameters anywhere in the seed files
RelationConfig is a Java record with fully typed fields; PosGroup enum includes a fromString() factory that guards against invalid string values
Model classes (ExplorationResult, ComparisonResult, AdjectiveProfile) expose final typed fields with no Object or unchecked types
type_discipline.issue_character: The issues are a recurring pattern of nullable-String returns that bypass Optional, localised to public-facing utility and model methods, plus one enum bypass in TaggedToken where the PosGroup type system was extended via raw strings rather than enum entries.
type_discipline.score_rationale: The codebase sits solidly in the 60–70 band. Generics and records are used well throughout, and Optional appears where it matters most (getRelation). However, several public methods (getStrongestNoun, requireParam, getFullPattern, getDeprel) return String while silently returning null, defeating the type system in the same way @Nullable-unannotated Java code does. The TaggedToken.getPosGroup() enum bypass is the most structurally problematic issue: an enum exists but five categories simply fall back to raw literal strings, making PosGroup.fromString() a lossy round-trip. These are not catastrophic but they are consistent enough to pull the score below 65.

strengths: Naming is uniformly standard Java camelCase/PascalCase/UPPER_CASE across all 32 files — a developer sees zero naming-style drift between packages.
api/ package applies a consistent encapsulation pattern: handler classes are package-private, only the server facade and stateless utilities are public.
Utility classes (CqlUtils, PatternSubstitution, LogDiceCalculator, QueryResults) uniformly use private constructors and static methods — one clear pattern throughout.
Package decomposition is logical and self-explanatory (model, api, config, query, exploration, indexer/blacklab, tagging, utils, viz) — a developer can predict where to look.
BlackLab indexer correctly isolates its implementation in a subdirectory (indexer/blacklab/), establishing a good convention that other packages could follow.
convention_outlier.issue_character: Convention drift is bounded to two areas — type-strategy inconsistency within the model package (public-fields vs. getter-based vs. record) and a structural divergence between how the indexer and the query packages organise their BlackLab-specific implementations.
convention_outlier.score_rationale: The codebase sits above the 70-point 'mixed' threshold because naming is fully consistent and most packages follow clear, uniform patterns. However, a developer working in the model layer will immediately face a 'which pattern do I follow?' question when ComparisonResult uses getters while five sibling classes expose public fields, and ExploreOptions is a record — this is genuine friction that places the score below 80. The indexer/query structural divergence adds a second point of confusion for orientation without being widespread enough to drop into the 60s.

strengths: All synchronous — no mixed sync/async in the public API surface
requireParam and parseExploreParams are consistent helpers applied across explore handlers, reducing per-endpoint variance for parameter extraction
Error responses all use a uniform JSON shape: {"error": "..."}  via HttpApiUtils.sendError
The four explore-adjacent endpoints share buildBaseExploreResponse, so the core response envelope (status, relation_type, parameters) is always shaped the same
api_surface_coherence.issue_character: Issues are structural and systemic rather than isolated: parallel endpoints consistently use different field names for equivalent data (patterns/relations, seeds/nouns), and response shapes are conditionally populated in ways that require defensive client-side coding.
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: The API has clear intent and consistent patterns within subsystems, but a developer writing a client against the full surface will encounter at least four naming divergences between related endpoints, a duplicate field, and conditionally present fields — all of which force client-side branching or inspection. These are not isolated quirks; they recur across the sketch, exploration, and concordance groups. This places the codebase squarely below the 'solid but uneven' range (80) and closer to 'significant drag' (60), as a developer must read implementation details to safely consume the API rather than rely on a coherent contract.

strengths: Model layer (AdjectiveProfile, ComparisonResult, CoreCollocate, ExplorationResult, Edge) uses direct, immutable final-field definitions with focused constructors and no leaking responsibilities
PosGroup enum and CqlUtils are textbook direct definitions: named constants, a clear factory, a single-purpose utility method, and Javadoc that states scope limits
QueryExecutor interface compensates for its surface complexity with explicit Javadoc that names the exact semantic difference between four similar-looking methods, letting callers choose correctly
ExplorationHandlers uses a private record ExploreParams and a single parseExploreParams helper to avoid duplicating tri-param extraction in two code paths — a direct, DRY definition
definition_directness.issue_character: Issues are concentrated in a small number of definition-layer tricks and parameter-retention compromises that introduce indirection or dishonesty to work around API-evolution constraints, rather than being a codebase-wide pattern.
definition_directness.score_rationale: The model and config layers are well-defined and honest; most classes say what they are and do what they say. However, three specific definition-level problems meaningfully pull the score down: the unused-but-retained parameters in QueryExecutor.executeSurfacePattern create an interface that actively misleads callers about its contract; the throwMissing trick in GrammarConfigLoader requires the reader to reason past an unexpected type guarantee; and RelationConfig.posGroupFromConstraint duplicates a positional mapping as chained string checks rather than a lookup table, obscuring its intent. These are isolated but structurally significant because they appear on heavily-used code paths. Against a 80=solid-but-uneven anchor, the strong model layer earns partial credit but the interface honesty gap and the positional-map pattern keep the overall score below solid.

strengths: Package structure cleanly separates api, config, exploration, model, query, indexer, tagging, utils, and viz — each has a coherent single responsibility with no cross-cutting concern mixing.
Model classes are honest data holders; no service logic leaks into them, and they carry appropriate utility methods (getEdges, getTopNouns) without becoming god-objects.
HttpApiUtils extracts all HTTP-level concerns into single-purpose static methods, letting handlers stay focused on domain logic.
WordSketchApiServer is a thin routing layer — it creates handlers, registers routes, and provides error-wrapping; all domain decisions live elsewhere.
QueryExecutor interface distinguishes its methods precisely (findCollocations vs executeSurfacePattern vs executeBcqlQuery vs executeDependencyPattern) with javadoc explaining the distinctions.
design_coherence.issue_character: Issues are concentrated at the API-to-service boundary: two HTTP handlers duplicate a structural response-building pattern, one interface carries dead parameters that every call site must stub, and one handler pulls domain assembly logic (pattern construction, options building) that belongs in the service layer — all pointing to an integration seam that was not fully abstracted.
design_coherence.score_rationale: The overall architecture is sound and well-decomposed; a developer navigating any single layer finds focused, readable code. What pulls the score down is a pattern of leaky abstraction at the exploration API boundary: the duplicate for-loops across two handlers are mechanical noise that a shared helper would eliminate, and the 'currently unused' interface parameters represent an honest-interface failure that affects the public contract of QueryExecutor. These are real structural problems, not style preferences, but they are isolated to one subsystem rather than pervasive. Against the global anchors this lands around 72: clearly above the 'significant drag' threshold (60) because the codebase is largely well-structured, but short of 'solid but uneven' (80) because the integration seam shows a consistent pattern of incomplete abstraction rather than one-off lapses.

strengths: WordSketchApiServer.wrapHandler provides a uniform last-resort safety net for the entire API layer, ensuring no unhandled exception leaks to the HTTP client
BlackLabQueryExecutor is internally consistent: every public method declares throws IOException, and every third-party exception is wrapped with cause chain preserved
GrammarConfigLoader validates config thoroughly and always throws IOException with specific diagnostic messages, never returning null on failure
error_consistency.issue_character: The issues are a mix of inconsistency within the API handler layer (some handlers catch locally with domain messages, others silently delegate to the safety net) and one genuine silent-swallow in the full-sketch relation loop that returns partial results without any client signal.
error_consistency.score_rationale: The codebase has a solid safety net and consistent patterns in its infrastructure layer (query execution, config loading), which prevents the worst failures. However, the API handler layer shows real mixed strategies: exploreMultiSeed wraps IOException explicitly while sibling handlers (exploreByPattern, compareCollocateProfiles, fetchExamples) do not, and handleFullSketchImpl swallows per-relation failures silently producing partial results the caller cannot detect. This places the codebase at the lower end of the 'mixed' range (70) — a developer reading one handler cannot predict how the next one handles errors, and the silent-swallow finding is a genuine reliability concern that pulls the score below 70.

strengths: Handler classes (SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers) add genuine value at each layer: parameter validation, error wrapping, JSON serialisation, and response shaping — not mere forwarding.
WordSketchApiServer delegates cleanly to SketchHandlers and ExplorationHandlers while retaining ownership of server lifecycle and route registration.
The mechanical scan found zero delegation-heavy classes, confirming the absence of widespread forwarding patterns across the codebase.

CollocateProfileComparator was intentionally extracted for size reasons and owns its logic fully — the class boundary carries real cohesion, even if SemanticFieldExplorer's relay methods dilute that benefit.
delegation_density.issue_character: Isolated, architecturally motivated relay methods in SemanticFieldExplorer that forward to an inner collaborator with zero added value; the pattern is small in scope but visible because SemanticFieldExplorer is the main facade callers use.
delegation_density.score_rationale: The codebase is largely free of delegation-heavy patterns — the mechanical detector found nothing, and most class boundaries carry real behavior. The score is pulled below 80 by two pure pass-through public methods in SemanticFieldExplorer (compareCollocateProfiles and exploreMultiSeed) that make the class a partial relay, and by a trivial private relay in RelationConfig. These are localized issues in an otherwise direct codebase, placing it in the 70-80 band: better than 'significant drag' (60) but short of 'solid but uneven' (80) because the SemanticFieldExplorer facade pattern is a concrete structural choice that obscures the real owner of the logic.

strengths: SemanticFieldExplorer is tested with a well-designed in-memory StubExecutor covering 8 meaningful cases including edge cases (null seeds, empty collocates, partial sharing, edge weights).
BlackLabSnippetParser has thorough unit tests covering null, empty, multi-token, and attribute-fallback cases.
Utility classes (LogDice, LongIntHashMap, PatternSubstitution, SimpleTagger) are well-exercised.
Grammar/config layer is covered by three test classes (GrammarConfigLoaderTest, RelationsValidationTest, HybridAlgorithmTest) including both inline JSON and default grammar.
HandlersTest correctly uses a lightweight MockExchange (no real server) to test input-validation rejection paths.
test_strategy.issue_character: Coverage collapses precisely at the infrastructure boundary: everything that requires BlackLab (the core query engine, indexer, E2E pipeline) is either fully disabled with empty-body placeholder classes or relies on hardcoded developer-local paths that silently skip on any other machine.
test_strategy.score_rationale: The codebase scores well for units that can be tested in isolation (parsing, utilities, config, exploration logic), showing the team knows how to write good unit tests. However, the critical path — BlackLabQueryExecutor, the indexer, and the full HTTP handler pipeline — has essentially zero verified test coverage: BlackLabQueryExecutorTest is a disabled empty shell, five other suites are disabled placeholders, and the few integration-style tests that do exist are gated by hardcoded Windows developer paths. This is a significant structural gap: the core corpus query engine (490 lines, scoreHits, findCollocations, getTotalFrequency) runs untested in CI, meaning regressions in the most complex business logic cannot be caught automatically. The gap between the well-tested periphery and the entirely untested core pushes the score well below 50.

issues.summary: CLAUDE.md API table documents only 4 of 14 registered endpoints, misrepresenting the public API surface
issues.suggestion: Expand the CLAUDE.md API endpoint table to list all 14 registered endpoints, matching the class-level Javadoc on WordSketchApiServer. Alternatively, replace the partial table with a reference to the Javadoc and keep CLAUDE.md focused on the two or three most commonly used entry points, making the incompleteness explicit.
evidence: CLAUDE.md lines 70-75: table lists only /health, /api/sketch/{lemma}, /api/semantic-field/explore, /api/semantic-field/explore-multi.

WordSketchApiServer.start() registers 14 contexts including /api/relations, /api/relations/dep, /api/semantic-field, /api/semantic-field/examples, /api/concordance/examples, /api/visual/radial (GET+POST), and /api/bcql — none of these appear in CLAUDE.md.
WordSketchApiServer class Javadoc does list all 14 endpoints, but developers consulting CLAUDE.md as the project guide get an incomplete picture.

issues.summary: handleSemanticField silently accepts both 'seeds' and 'nouns' as query parameters with no documentation, blurring the /api/semantic-field endpoint's contract
issues.suggestion: Pick a single canonical parameter name for /api/semantic-field ('nouns' is semantically correct for comparison mode) and document it. If backward compatibility with 'seeds' must be kept, add an explicit comment and document the alias in CLAUDE.md. Alternatively, consolidate the alias into HttpApiUtils.requireParam with a documented fallback variant.
evidence: ExplorationHandlers.java line 259: 'String nounsParam = params.containsKey("seeds") ? params.get("seeds") : HttpApiUtils.requireParam(exchange, params, "nouns");'
The 'seeds' alias appears to be a compatibility shim for callers using the explore-endpoint parameter naming convention, but neither CLAUDE.md nor inline Javadoc documents it.
The endpoint's canonical concept is a noun comparison set; 'seeds' conflates it with the single-seed exploration vocabulary, making the parameter contract ambiguous.

issues.summary: SemanticFieldExplorer retains a deprecated String-path constructor alongside the preferred QueryExecutor injection constructor, leaving two competing initialization contracts
issues.suggestion: Remove the deprecated constructor now that WordSketchApiServer uses the injection path exclusively. If removal is deferred, at minimum add a @SuppressWarnings boundary in the one test that still constructs via path, so the deprecation warning surface is explicit and bounded.
evidence: SemanticFieldExplorer.java: @Deprecated public SemanticFieldExplorer(String indexPath) — creates an owned BlackLabQueryExecutor internally.
Preferred constructor: SemanticFieldExplorer(QueryExecutor) — injected executor, no ownership.

The class-level Javadoc on the deprecated constructor says 'Prefer SemanticFieldExplorer(QueryExecutor) to avoid double-open of the same index (issue 297b2b52)' — the retained path carries a known correctness risk.
WordSketchApiServer already uses the preferred constructor (new SemanticFieldExplorer(executor)), so the deprecated path has no active callers in production.

issues.summary: CollocateProfileComparator is a mechanical class-split from SemanticFieldExplorer to satisfy a line-count threshold, not a genuine domain abstraction
issues.suggestion: Inline CollocateProfileComparator back into SemanticFieldExplorer (or a dedicated MultiSeedExplorer class with a real domain name), removing the duplicated constant. If SemanticFieldExplorer is genuinely too long, split by single-seed vs multi-seed responsibility rather than by line count.
evidence: CollocateProfileComparator Javadoc: 'Extracted from SemanticFieldExplorer to keep that class below the 400-line threshold'
ADJECTIVE_PATTERN constant '[xpos="JJ.*"]' is duplicated verbatim in both SemanticFieldExplorer (line ~84) and CollocateProfileComparator (line ~18)
CollocateProfileComparator is only ever instantiated once, inside SemanticFieldExplorer's constructors — it has no independent users
SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreMultiSeed is a single-line pure delegation: 'return comparator.exploreMultiSeed(seeds, relationConfig, minLogDice, topCollocates, minShared)'

issues.summary: ExplorationHandlers stores a QueryExecutor field that is never called, making the constructor dependency invisible dead cargo
issues.suggestion: Remove the QueryExecutor parameter from ExplorationHandlers constructor and the corresponding field. The SemanticFieldExplorer already encapsulates executor access; ExplorationHandlers needs only grammarConfig and semanticFieldExplorer.
evidence: ExplorationHandlers constructor (line 39) accepts QueryExecutor and assigns 'this.executor = executor'
No call site in ExplorationHandlers invokes 'executor.<anyMethod>' — all execution goes through 'this.semanticFieldExplorer'

WordSketchApiServer passes 'executor' to ExplorationHandlers constructor (alongside a SemanticFieldExplorer already constructed with the same executor) — the double-passing is the symptom of the dead field

issues.summary: SemanticFieldExplorer.indexPath is always null in the primary (non-deprecated) construction path and is never read
issues.suggestion: Remove the indexPath field entirely. The deprecated constructor can be simplified or removed; if retained for backward compatibility, drop the field assignment since the value is never used.
evidence: Preferred DI constructor (line 103): 'this.indexPath = null' — indexPath is set to null and never read in any method
indexPath is only assigned in the @Deprecated String-path constructor (line 95): 'this.indexPath = indexPath'
No method in SemanticFieldExplorer reads 'this.indexPath' after construction

issues.summary: ExplorationResult exposes all state as public fields while sibling ComparisonResult uses private fields with getters — inconsistent model encapsulation with no documented rationale
issues.suggestion: Align ExplorationResult with ComparisonResult by making fields private and adding accessor methods (or converting to a record). This decouples callers from the internal storage shape and makes both result types consistent.
evidence: ExplorationResult declares: 'public final String seed; public final Map<String, Double> seedCollocates; public final Map<String, Long> seedCollocateFrequencies; public final List<DiscoveredNoun> discoveredNouns; public final List<CoreCollocate> coreCollocates'
ComparisonResult declares 'private final List<String> nouns; private final List<AdjectiveProfile> adjectives' with getters getNouns() / getAllAdjectives()
ExplorationHandlers accesses 'result.seed', 'result.seedCollocates', 'result.discoveredNouns' directly via field access — the abstraction boundary between result and consumer is absent

issues.summary: QueryExecutor.executeSurfacePattern declares headPosition and collocatePosition parameters documented as 'currently unused', leaking implementation detail through the interface contract
issues.suggestion: Remove headPosition and collocatePosition from the interface signature (they are not used) and derive collocate position internally in the implementation. If future implementations genuinely need positional hints, add them back with a concrete use case.
evidence: QueryExecutor.executeSurfacePattern Javadoc: 'headPosition 1-based position of the head token (currently unused)' and 'collocatePosition 1-based position of the collocate token (currently unused)'
BlackLabQueryExecutor.executeSurfacePattern ignores both int parameters and derives collocate position from the pattern label instead
The parameters exist for 'interface compatibility' per the Javadoc — compatibility with no other current implementation

issues.summary: handleSemanticFieldExplore and handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti share ~80% of their body with no unified extraction beyond a small helper
issues.suggestion: Extract a private buildExploreResponse(ExplorationResult, Map<String,Object>) helper that populates the four shared list sections; call it from both handlers after they set their seed-specific keys. The validator+param-parse block can also be unified into parseExploreParams already present, reducing each handler to ~20 lines of seed-specific logic.
evidence: Both methods contain identical grammar-config null guard, relation lookup, ExploreParams parsing, and the four formatXxx/response.put loops for seed_collocates, core_collocates, discovered_nouns, and edges
buildBaseExploreResponse was extracted but the surrounding parameter-validation and result-assembly blocks (the majority of each method) were not
The only substantive differences are: single String seed vs Set<String> seeds extraction, the explorer call, and two extra response keys in the multi-seed variant

issues.summary: SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern accumulates four mutation phases inline with entangled mutable state rather than delegating to named sub-methods
issues.suggestion: Extract the four phases into private methods analogous to CollocateProfileComparator: e.g. fetchSeedCollocates(seed, bcqlPattern, simplePattern, ...), buildNounProfiles(seedCollocScores, ...), scoreDiscoveredNouns(nounProfiles, ...), identifyCoreCollocates(discoveredNouns, seedCollocScores, ...). Each returns its result type, eliminating shared mutable maps.
evidence: The method is ~140 LOC and builds up seedCollocScores, nounProfiles, collocFrequency, collocTotalScore, discoveredNouns, and coreCollocates in sequential loops with no helper extraction

By contrast, CollocateProfileComparator (extracted from the same class) decomposes its analogous logic into buildRawAdjectiveProfiles and buildAdjectiveProfileList, demonstrating that the author knew the pattern but did not apply it back here
The fallback-to-simple-pattern block (two sequential calls to executor with a null-check between them) adds an additional branch not reflected in the method's name or javadoc

issues.summary: BlackLabQueryExecutor.collectHits uses nested null-guards that make the normal execution path hard to distinguish from error paths
issues.suggestion: Replace the nested null-guard with an early-continue guard: 'if (conc == null) continue;' and 'if (parts == null || parts.length < 3) { logger.warn("..."); continue; }'. This makes the happy path linear and each failure mode explicit.
evidence: Variables leftText, matchText, rightText, xmlSnippet are initialised to empty string then only assigned inside 'if (conc != null)' → 'if (parts != null && parts.length >= 3)', meaning a null concordance silently produces an all-empty HitRecord
The final 'if (collocateLemma != null && !collocateLemma.isEmpty())' merge still runs for the empty-concordance case, adding a no-op entry to records
The two-level null guard (concordance null, then parts null) conflates two distinct failure modes without separate logging or early-continue

issues.summary: handleSemanticField silently accepts an empty 'seeds' parameter, bypassing the requireParam guard that protects the 'nouns' parameter
issues.suggestion: After the ternary, add: 'if (nounsParam == null || nounsParam.isBlank()) { HttpApiUtils.sendError(exchange, 400, "Missing required parameter: nouns or seeds"); return; }' to enforce the same non-empty contract regardless of which alias was used.
evidence: Line: 'String nounsParam = params.containsKey("seeds") ? params.get("seeds") : HttpApiUtils.requireParam(exchange, params, "nouns");'

If ?seeds= is present but empty, params.get("seeds") returns an empty string; the 'if (nounsParam == null) return;' check passes, and Arrays.asList("".split(",")) produces a Set containing one empty string
requireParam enforces non-empty validation, but that guard is only reached when 'seeds' key is absent entirely

issues.summary: BlackLabSnippetParser retains an @Deprecated extractCollocateFromMatchText method annotated as having no production callers
issues.suggestion: Delete the method. If historical traceability is needed, a one-line comment referencing the commit that removed it is sufficient. Verify no test-only callers exist first.
evidence: Method extractCollocateFromMatchText at line 145 carries @Deprecated and javadoc 'No production callers'
The method operates on a plain-text space-separated token model that is no longer used; all active callers use XML-based extraction
Its presence adds confusion about whether position-based extraction should use this method or extractLemmaAt/extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition

issues.summary: ExplorationResult→JSON translation is duplicated across two explore handlers instead of centralised
issues.suggestion: Add a toResponseMap() or a dedicated ExplorationResultSerializer class that translates ExplorationResult into its canonical Map<String,Object> representation. Both handlers call that single translation; the multi-seed handler can layer its 'common_collocates' (strings) field on top of the shared base rather than re-iterating coreCollocates.
evidence: handleSemanticFieldExplore and handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti each contain independent loops over result.seedCollocates, result.coreCollocates, and result.getEdges() using the same formatSeedCollocate/formatCoreCollocate/formatEdge helpers.

handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti iterates result.coreCollocates twice: once to build 'common_collocates' (strings) and again to build 'core_collocates' (full maps), adding ad-hoc boundary logic that does not exist in the single-seed path.
Both handlers share a ~30-line block of response assembly that differs only in a few endpoint-specific fields; any change to ExplorationResult serialisation must be made in two places.

issues.summary: Handlers null-check ExplorationResult fields that ExplorationResult guarantees are non-null
issues.suggestion: Make ExplorationResult's fields explicitly non-null by contract (add @NonNull annotations or enforce in the constructor with Objects.requireNonNull). Remove the null guards in the handlers. If a null-safe path is ever needed, let ExplorationResult.empty() be the canonical representation and rely on it.
evidence: ExplorationResult.empty() initialises seedCollocates with Map.of(), seedCollocateFrequencies with Map.of(), discoveredNouns with List.of(), and coreCollocates with List.of() — none can be null from any constructor path.

Both handleSemanticFieldExplore and handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti guard every iteration with 'if (result.seedCollocates != null)', 'if (result.discoveredNouns != null)', 'if (result.coreCollocates != null)', 'if (result.getEdges() != null)' — treating non-null guarantees as optional.
The inconsistency means the model's contract is not enforced: callers must mentally track whether the guarantee holds rather than relying on the type.

issues.summary: CQL POS regex pattern derived from PosGroup enum inside an HTTP handler rather than RelationConfig
issues.suggestion: Add a reverseCollocatePattern() method to RelationConfig (or PosGroup) that returns the appropriate CQL constraint string for the collocate's POS group. The handler simply calls relationConfig.get().reverseCollocatePattern() and passes it through.
evidence: In handleSemanticFieldExplore: String reverseCollocatePattern = relationConfig.get().collocatePosGroup() == PosGroup.ADJ ? "[xpos=\"JJ.*\"]" : "[xpos=\"NN.*|VB.*\"]"; — the HTTP handler contains a switch over grammar domain knowledge.

RelationConfig already owns collocatePosGroup() and all POS-pattern logic (posGroupFromConstraint, extractLabelContent); the PosGroup→CQL-regex step is a natural extension of that existing responsibility.
This coupling means a change to the xpos prefix for adjectives (e.g., for a different corpus tagset) would require updating an HTTP handler instead of the grammar config layer.

issues.summary: handleConcordanceExamples silently substitutes a proximity query when the requested relation is absent
issues.suggestion: Replace the silent fallback with an explicit 400 error when the relation is unknown. If a fallback proximity search is intentionally supported, include a 'fallback': true field and a 'fallback_reason': '...' field in the response so callers can distinguish the two cases.
evidence: When grammarConfig is null or the relation is not found, the handler constructs a fallback: bcqlQuery = String.format('"word1" []{0,5} "word2"', ...) and proceeds to execute it.

The JSON response includes 'relation': the original relation ID requested, 'bcql': the fallback proximity pattern, and 'status': 'ok' — there is no 'fallback': true or warning field to inform the caller that a semantically different search was performed.
The caller asked for a relation-specific concordance (e.g., noun_adj_predicates) but receives proximity co-occurrence matches with no indication of the substitution.

issues.summary: Nullable grammarConfig propagates to multiple handlers instead of failing fast at startup
issues.suggestion: Require grammarConfig to be non-null in WordSketchApiServer (throw IllegalArgumentException or NullPointerException in the constructor). Remove the per-handler null guards. If a 'no grammar' mode is genuinely needed, introduce a NullGrammarConfig object or an Optional<GrammarConfigLoader> with a clearly named absent-config policy.
evidence: WordSketchApiServer constructor accepts a nullable grammarConfig and only logs a warning: 'WordSketchApiServer initialized without grammar configuration'.

handleSemanticFieldExplore, handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti, and handleRelationsImpl each independently check 'if (grammarConfig == null) { sendError 500 }' — the same lifecycle guard is repeated three times across two classes.
Partial-initialisation state is observable only at request time, not at startup, making it harder to detect misconfiguration during server startup or in tests.

issues.summary: AdjectiveProfile.getStrongestNoun() returns String but can return null via .orElse(null)
issues.suggestion: Change the return type to Optional<String> and update all callers to handle the Optional explicitly (e.g., .ifPresent(n -> adjMap.put("specific_to", n)))
evidence: AdjectiveProfile.getStrongestNoun() line 48: .orElse(null) makes null a valid return, but the declared return type is String

ExplorationHandlers.formatAdjectiveProfile() calls adj.getStrongestNoun() and puts the result into a map as adjMap.put("specific_to", adj.getStrongestNoun()) without a null check — a null value would silently enter the JSON response

issues.summary: TaggedToken.getPosGroup() returns raw string literals for five POS categories not covered by the PosGroup enum
issues.suggestion: Either (a) extend PosGroup with DET, PRON, PREP, CONJ, PUNCT entries and return PosGroup from getPosGroup(), or (b) collapse all non-NOUN/VERB/ADJ/ADV cases to PosGroup.OTHER and return the enum type; callers that need fine-grained categories can call getTag() directly
evidence: TaggedToken.getPosGroup() returns "det", "pron", "prep", "conj", "punct" as raw String literals for D/P/I/C/U first-character POS tags

PosGroup enum defines only NOUN, VERB, ADJ, ADV, OTHER; PosGroup.fromString() maps none of the five raw literals back to an enum constant, returning OTHER for all of them — making the values unreachable via the enum type

issues.summary: ExplorationHandlers casts Object to Map<String, Object> without a type-safe alternative
issues.suggestion: Capture the parameters sub-map in a local variable before inserting it into the response: Map<String, Object> paramsMap = new HashMap<>(); ... response.put("parameters", paramsMap); then reuse paramsMap for subsequent .put() calls, eliminating the cast entirely
evidence: Line 100: ((Map<String, Object>) response.get("parameters")).put("nouns_per", nounsPerCollocate) — response is Map<String, Object>, so .get() returns Object; the cast is unchecked and will produce a compiler warning
The buildBaseExploreResponse helper stores the parameters sub-map as a plain Object entry, losing the type information immediately after construction

issues.summary: RelationConfig.relationType record field can hold null because parseRelationType() returns null on absent or invalid input
issues.suggestion: Either (a) make relationType non-nullable by defaulting to RelationType.SURFACE in parseRelationType() instead of returning null, or (b) declare the field as Optional<RelationType> and guard all usages; option (a) is simpler and matches how the field is used in practice
evidence: GrammarConfigLoader.parseRelationType() lines 205-207: returns null for a blank/absent value and for an unrecognised enum name
RelationConfig record declares 'RelationType relationType' without @NonNull or Optional; the field is thus silently nullable
SketchHandlers line 95: rel.relationType().name() is called without a null guard — if any relation in the config omits 'relation_type', this throws NullPointerException at runtime

issues.summary: ComparisonResult uses private fields + getters while 5 sibling model classes use public final fields
issues.suggestion: Pick one pattern for the model/ package and apply it uniformly. The simplest migration is to convert ComparisonResult to use public final fields matching its siblings, replacing getNouns() with direct access to a public final List<String> nouns field, and so on. Alternatively, convert all data-holder classes to Java records (AdjectiveProfile, ComparisonResult, CoreCollocate, DiscoveredNoun, Edge, ExplorationResult) for immutability and conciseness — ExploreOptions already demonstrates this approach.
evidence: AdjectiveProfile, CoreCollocate, DiscoveredNoun, Edge, ExplorationResult all declare public final fields and expose data directly (e.g., adj.presentInCount, dn.sharedCount, ca.collocate).
ComparisonResult declares private final fields and exposes them via getters: getNouns(), getAllAdjectives(), getFullyShared(), getPartiallyShared(), getSpecific(), getSpecificTo(), getEdges().
ExploreOptions is a Java record — a third distinct pattern for value-holder classes in the same package.

ExplorationHandlers directly accesses public fields on DiscoveredNoun (dn.noun, dn.sharedCount) and calls getters on ComparisonResult (result.getAllAdjectives(), result.getFullyShared()), making the inconsistency visible to every caller.

issues.summary: BlackLab query implementation sits at query/ root; indexer correctly isolates its BlackLab code in indexer/blacklab/
issues.suggestion: Move BlackLabQueryExecutor and BlackLabSnippetParser into a new query/blacklab/ subpackage (package pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch.query.blacklab), mirroring the indexer/blacklab/ convention. QueryExecutor remains in query/ as the interface. Update the two import sites (WordSketchApiServer, SemanticFieldExplorer).
evidence: indexer/blacklab/ contains BlackLabConllUIndexer and ConlluConverter — the BlackLab implementation is isolated from any future non-BlackLab indexer.

query/ contains QueryExecutor (interface), BlackLabQueryExecutor, and BlackLabSnippetParser at the same level — the interface and its sole implementation are peers, not separated as in the indexer package.

A developer adding a second query backend (e.g., a Lucene-direct executor) would follow the indexer convention and create query/lucene/ or query/blacklab/, but there is no existing signal in the query/ package that this is the expected pattern.

issues.summary: ADJECTIVE_PATTERN constant duplicated in two sibling classes in the exploration/ package
issues.suggestion: Extract shared pattern constants into a package-private constants class in the exploration/ package (e.g., ExplorationPatterns with public static final fields ADJECTIVE_PATTERN and NOUN_PATTERN). Both SemanticFieldExplorer and CollocateProfileComparator reference the shared constants.
evidence: SemanticFieldExplorer defines: private static final String ADJECTIVE_PATTERN = "[xpos=\"JJ.*\"]";
CollocateProfileComparator defines: private static final String ADJECTIVE_PATTERN = "[xpos=\"JJ.*\"]"; — identical string in the same package.
NOUN_PATTERN is also defined in SemanticFieldExplorer but not in CollocateProfileComparator, suggesting the duplication is accidental rather than an intentional separation of concerns.

issues.summary: GET /api/sketch/{lemma} uses 'patterns' key while GET /api/sketch/{lemma}/dep uses 'relations' for equivalent sketch data
issues.suggestion: Use a single top-level key (e.g., 'sketch') for both surface and dependency responses; distinguish the type via the already-present 'type' field (which is only set for DEP today). Alternatively, unify both as 'relations' and always emit 'type'.
evidence: handleFullSketchImpl: when relationType==SURFACE, puts result as response.put("patterns", byRelation); when relationType==DEP, puts response.put("relations", byRelation) (lines 175-178)
A client must branch on the presence of 'patterns' vs 'relations' to navigate the same conceptual data

issues.summary: GET /api/semantic-field accepts both 'seeds' and 'nouns' as aliases for the same input parameter
issues.suggestion: Standardise on 'seeds' across all exploration endpoints and remove the 'nouns' alias; if backward compatibility matters, document the alias explicitly and deprecate it.
evidence: handleSemanticField: String nounsParam = params.containsKey("seeds") ? params.get("seeds") : HttpApiUtils.requireParam(exchange, params, "nouns") (lines 259-261)
The explore endpoints use 'seed' / 'seeds', and the comparison endpoint accepts 'seeds' silently before requiring 'nouns', creating an undocumented alias

issues.summary: Multi-seed explore response includes both 'seeds' (input list) and 'seed' (internal ExplorationResult.seed) at the same level
issues.suggestion: Remove 'seed' from the multi-seed response or rename it 'representative_seed' with documentation; it should not shadow the canonical 'seeds' field.
evidence: handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti: response.put("seeds", new ArrayList<>(seeds)) followed immediately by response.put("seed", result.seed) (lines 198-200)
A consumer cannot tell whether 'seed' is one of the input seeds or a derived representative; single-seed explore only emits 'seed'

issues.summary: Concordance examples response emits 'highlighted' with a value identical to 'sentence', adding API surface noise
issues.suggestion: Either remove 'highlighted' if it provides no additional value, or populate it with actual highlight markup (e.g., offset-based HTML). As-is it misleads clients into expecting a distinct format.
evidence: exMap.put("sentence", r.getSentence()) and exMap.put("highlighted", r.getSentence()) both call getSentence() with no transformation (lines 297-298)
The 'highlighted' field implies markup or annotation, but it contains the same plain text
